name: Loogaroo
description: The Loogaroo, also known as Lougarou or Ole Higue, is a terrifying creature from Caribbean folklore, particularly prevalent in Trinidad and Tobago, Dominica, and other islands. Originating from French loup-garou (werewolf), the Loogaroo is a shape-shifting hag, often an old woman by day, who sheds her skin at night to transform into a ball of fire or other monstrous form. She then flies through the air, entering homes through cracks and crevices to suck the blood of her victims, especially babies. Protection against the Loogaroo includes scattering rice or salt at doorways (which she is compelled to count), and turning clothes inside out. The creature represents anxieties surrounding nocturnal dangers, female power, and the vulnerability of children. It serves as a cautionary tale and a means of explaining unexplained illnesses and misfortunes. Some believe the Loogaroo gains her power through dark magic and rituals, passed down through generations, making her a constant threat to unsuspecting villagers. Her ability to remove her skin and store it safely before transforming is a particularly gruesome and unsettling aspect of the myth.
subtitle: Caribbean blood-sucking fire hag
appearance: By day, the Loogaroo appears as an elderly woman, often unassuming and frail. At night, she sheds her skin, revealing a raw, bloody form or transforms into a ball of fire. Some accounts describe her as having sharp claws and teeth, and glowing red eyes when in her monstrous state.
habitat: Rural villages and isolated homes in the Caribbean islands, particularly in areas with strong oral traditions and beliefs in folklore.
behavior: The Loogaroo is a nocturnal predator, preying on vulnerable individuals, especially babies. She is cunning and secretive, blending in with ordinary people during the day. She performs rituals to maintain her powers and carefully hides her shed skin. She is driven by a thirst for blood and a desire to cause harm.

keyMyths.title: Shedding the Skin
keyMyths.description: The Loogaroo must carefully remove her skin and store it in a mortar filled with salt before transforming. If someone finds and salts the skin, she will be unable to return to human form and will die at sunrise.

keyMyths.title: Counting Grains
keyMyths.description: Loogaroos are compelled to count grains of rice or salt scattered in their path. This distracts them, giving potential victims time to protect themselves or escape.

keyMyths.title: Ole Higue's Curse
keyMyths.description: Becoming a Loogaroo often involves a pact with dark forces or inheriting the curse from a family member. This explains the creature's insatiable hunger and malicious intent.

sources.author: J. Elder
sources.work: Erzulie's Daughters: Black Women and Power in the Caribbean
sources.description: Explores the socio-cultural context of female supernatural figures in Caribbean mythology.
sources.text: Explores the socio-cultural context of female supernatural figures in Caribbean mythology.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Collection of folktales and myths passed down through generations.
sources.text: Collection of folktales and myths passed down through generations.
